title: Conditional Heavy Light Compute
research_question: Can a small model improve its loss-compute trade by spending expensive attention and FFN compute only on routed important tokens instead of treating every token equally?
implementation_notes: Implemented via TOKEN_SELECTIVE_* env knobs that route top-k tokens into heavy FFN and/or attention branches.
The light attention path uses a cheap mixer-style update; the light FFN path uses the existing no-expand polynomial family.
This is a first pass at token-selective compute, not a full MoD-style training stack.

candidates.name: ac1-top-only-heavy-ffn-routing
candidates.goal: Test whether only a small subset of top-layer tokens needs full FFN refinement.
candidates.hypothesis: Top-only selective FFN compute can preserve quality while avoiding uniform heavy refinement at the very top.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether the most expensive token-wise compute should be concentrated only at the output-facing stage.
candidates.why_worthy: This is the cleanest first test of token-selective compute in the part of the stack where refinement matters most.
candidates.description: route top-layer tokens into heavy FFNs while all tokens get a lighter polynomial FFN path

candidates.name: ac2-full-upper-heavy-ffn-routing
candidates.goal: Test whether the whole upper reasoning band wants selective FFN compute, not just the final two layers.
candidates.hypothesis: The upper stack may benefit from repeated token-selective FFN refinement rather than uniform FFNs everywhere.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether selective FFN compute should be a top-only trick or an upper-stack design principle.
candidates.why_worthy: This distinguishes a narrow top refinement story from a broader token-selective upper-stack regime.
candidates.description: route upper-stack tokens into heavy FFNs while keeping a lighter FFN path for everyone

candidates.name: ac3-heavy-attention-on-routed-tokens
candidates.goal: Test whether only a subset of upper-stack tokens needs full global attention.
candidates.hypothesis: Many tokens may only need a cheap local-ish update, while a routed minority needs full global attention.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether selective attention is more important than selective FFN compute.
candidates.why_worthy: This directly questions the assumption that every upper-stack token deserves the same global reasoning budget.
candidates.description: use a light mixer path for all upper tokens and add full attention only for routed important tokens

candidates.name: ac4-joint-heavy-token-path
candidates.goal: Test whether the right abstraction is a full heavy-token path rather than separate heavy attention or heavy FFN decisions.
candidates.hypothesis: The model may want a small subset of important tokens to receive both heavy attention and heavy FFN updates together.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether token-selective compute should be understood as a coherent heavy-path routing mechanism.
candidates.why_worthy: This is the boldest and most faithful version of the conditional-compute question in one tranche.
candidates.description: give routed upper-stack tokens both heavy attention and heavy FFN updates while everyone else follows the light path

candidates.name: ac5-heuristic-proxy-routing
candidates.goal: Test whether selective allocation itself matters even before a learned router is optimized well.
candidates.hypothesis: If token-selective compute is the right principle, a crude importance proxy like token norm should still show some signal.
candidates.what_it_teaches: Whether the main gain comes from the allocation idea or only from a learned router.
candidates.why_worthy: This de-risks the interpretation of failures by separating routing quality from the heavy/light compute idea itself.
candidates.description: use norm-based heuristic token routing for the joint heavy-token path
